summary: DHH advocates for wikis over word processors, citing his own experience using Instiki and LaTeX for his bachelor's degree. He quotes KDE developer Richard Dale's puzzlement over why wikis haven't replaced word processors for most people. DHH attributes continued word processor usage to inertia, ignorance, or Stockholm Syndrome, and encourages readers to try Instiki as an alternative.
key_points: Wikis like Instiki are simpler and more powerful than word processors for collaborative writing
Most people rarely print documents, removing the primary advantage of traditional word processors
DHH successfully used Instiki and LaTeX for collaborative academic work while peers struggled with Word
Continued word processor usage is driven by inertia and familiarity rather than technical merit
money_quotes: Why do people still use word processors, rather than wikis, when they rarely print anything out on paper?
We used to have a good laugh at the other study groups still stuck struggling with Word.
Why would they slave so endlessly for that beast? Inertia, ignorance, Stockholm Syndrome?
key_insight: Word processors persist not because they're the best tool for collaborative writing, but because people default to familiar tools even when better alternatives exist.
